1. ## **Introduction** 

On Saturday, October 7, 2023, at 6:29 A.M., approximately 6,000 Hamas operatives crossed the Israel–Gaza border at 119 points along what Israel had constructed as a technologically advanced barrier system—often referred to as the "smart barrier" or "iron wall." The barrier incorporated layered defenses, including physical fencing, underground sensors, and surveillance infrastructure designed to detect and prevent infiltration. Shortly after crossing the border, forces from Hamas's elite Nukhba unit reached Re'im Base, the headquarters of Israel's Gaza Division, and disrupted its communication infrastructure (Meniv, 2024\).

Military organizations that lose their core command-and-control systems typically experience operational paralysis. Without the ability to receive situation reports, process information, and transmit orders, units on the ground often lack the coordination required to mount a coherent response (Spek & Anderson, 2022\). In such situations, operational effectiveness is expected to deteriorate until communication systems are restored.

On October 7, however, the Israeli response did not follow this pattern. Thousands of combat soldiers—including conscripts, reservists, police officers, and Shin Bet personnel—began moving toward the Gaza border shortly after the attack began. Many of these individuals did so without receiving formal orders, without functioning communication systems, and in some cases without full combat equipment. Despite these severe constraints, Israeli forces managed within several hours to halt the attack's expansion, regain control of most civilian communities, and prevent deeper penetration into Israeli territory.

Large-scale terrorist attacks create extreme stress tests not only for security forces but also for the administrative systems responsible for coordinating public response (Carter, 2001\). From a public administration perspective, such events provide rare opportunities to examine how public organizations maintain operational effectiveness when formal coordination mechanisms and communication infrastructures suddenly fail (Toth, 2025).[^1]

This paper asks what conditions allowed for an effective military response despite the collapse of centralized command and control. To address this question, the study introduces the concept of Embedded Response Capacity (ERC) and evaluates its explanatory power through empirical analysis of the October 7 events. ERC refers to the latent ability of trained actors within a security system to initiate coordinated responses during crises in which formal command structures and communication systems are disrupted. Rather than relying exclusively on hierarchical coordination, ERC emphasizes the distributed operational competence embedded within trained personnel across an organization.

In situating ERC within existing theoretical frameworks, this study draws on three complementary bodies of literature. First, research on high-reliability organizations (HRO) conceptualizes resilience as a set of organizing principles—including sensitivity to operations, commitment to resilience, and deference to expertise—that enable organizations to perform effectively under conditions of uncertainty (Weick et al., 1999; Weick & Sutcliffe, 2015). ERC can be understood as a field-level manifestation of these principles, emerging when operational actors exercise decentralized expertise during crises. Second, latent organizing theory emphasizes how preexisting teams, training structures, and professional networks can activate rapidly in response to emergencies even when centralized coordination is limited (Van Fenema & Romme, 2020). This perspective highlights the importance of institutional arrangements that embed response capabilities within organizations prior to crises. Third, research on resilient performance in defense and security organizations identifies individual-level capabilities—including adaptive decision-making, shared tactical knowledge, and mental resilience—as critical components of organizational effectiveness in extreme environments (Jones et al., 2022). Together, these literatures suggest that an organization's capacity to respond effectively to crises may depend not only on formal command structures but also on the distributed competencies embedded within its personnel.

Existing research on crisis governance and organizational resilience emphasizes leadership, coordination mechanisms, and institutional preparedness as key determinants of effective crisis response (Boin et al., 2017; Comfort, 2007; Moynihan, 2009). While this literature has significantly advanced understanding of how public organizations respond to extreme events, it focuses primarily on the functioning of formal command structures. ERC complements this perspective by highlighting how operational competence embedded within individuals and training systems can sustain organizational performance even when centralized coordination mechanisms are temporarily disrupted (Edri-Peer, Cohen, & Gilad, 2025).[^2]

Empirically, the study adopts a mixed-method research design combining quantitative analysis of original datasets with qualitative institutional analysis. The quantitative component relies on two newly constructed databases documenting the units that engaged Hamas forces during the October 7 battles and the security personnel fatalities recorded during the first two days of the attack. The qualitative component examines institutional characteristics of the Israeli security system—including the structure of the “people's army,” the integration of conscripts and reservists, and shared counterterrorism training frameworks—that may have generated Embedded Response Capacity.

Methodologically, the paper also introduces a novel approach to reconstructing battlefield participation using the casualty data as a proxy for mapping tactical mobilization under conditions of incomplete operational documentation. Such approaches are particularly valuable in the study of crisis response, where spontaneous mobilization and chaotic operational environments often leave incomplete records of responding forces.

The paper proceeds as follows. The next section examines the phenomenon of swarm-style terrorist attacks and the challenges they pose to conventional security institutions. The subsequent section reviews the literature on organizational resilience and crisis governance in public administration. The paper then develops a set of hypotheses derived from the ERC framework and presents the research design and datasets used to test them. The findings section reports the empirical results, followed by a discussion of the implications for theories of organizational resilience and crisis response in public institutions. The conclusion reflects on the broader relevance of Embedded Response Capacity for understanding how public organizations maintain effectiveness amid systemic disruption.

## **2\. Swarm Terrorism and the Limits of Centralized Command**

Public administration scholarship has long examined how public organizations respond to crises that disrupt normal coordination mechanisms and administrative routines.

In recent decades, violent non-state actors have increasingly adopted operational concepts designed to exploit the structural vulnerabilities of modern security organizations. One of the most significant of these concepts is **swarm attack**, a form of coordinated violence in which numerous small units strike multiple targets simultaneously across dispersed locations. Rather than concentrating force against a single objective, swarm attacks seek to overwhelm defensive systems by saturating them with concurrent threats.

The concept of swarming originally emerged in military theory to describe distributed operations in which many independent units converge on a target from multiple directions. Arquilla and Ronfeldt (2000) described swarming as a mode of conflict in which “dispersed units of a networked force converge on a target from multiple directions” in a coordinated but decentralized manner. While initially associated with network-centric warfare, the concept has increasingly been adopted by non-state actors who seek to compensate for their relative weakness in conventional combat.

Several major terrorist attacks illustrate the growing use of swarm tactics. The 2008 attacks in Mumbai involved ten militants operating in coordinated teams that struck multiple urban targets simultaneously, including hotels, a railway station, and a Jewish community center. The 2015 attacks in Paris similarly involved multiple teams conducting near-simultaneous assaults on entertainment venues, restaurants, and a sports stadium. In each case, the attackers relied on dispersion and simultaneity to stretch the response capacity of local security forces.

Swarm attacks create particular difficulties for security organizations that rely heavily on centralized command structures. Traditional command-and-control systems are designed to collect information from the field, synthesize it at headquarters, and distribute coordinated orders to subordinate units. This process functions effectively under conditions where events unfold at a pace that allows for deliberate decision-making. However, swarm attacks compress time and expand the number of simultaneous threats, often outpacing the ability of central command to process information and issue directives.

The challenge becomes even more severe when attackers deliberately target communication infrastructure. Modern security organizations increasingly depend on integrated surveillance systems, digital communications, and centralized command centers to maintain situational awareness. Disruptions to these systems—whether through cyber attack, electronic warfare, or physical sabotage—can significantly degrade command capabilities. When communication networks fail, the hierarchical structures that normally coordinate operations may instead become bottlenecks that delay response.

For this reason, much of the literature on command and control assumes that severe disruption to communication networks will produce **organizational paralysis**. Without reliable situational awareness or the ability to issue coordinated orders, frontline units may hesitate to act independently. The risk of misidentifying threats, duplicating efforts, or interfering with other units can discourage initiative. As a result, operational effectiveness may depend on the rapid restoration of communication links between headquarters and subordinate forces.

The events of October 7 appear to challenge this expectation. In the early stages of the attack, Hamas forces deliberately targeted Israel’s border surveillance and communication infrastructure. Sensors and observation systems along the Gaza border were destroyed, communication networks were disrupted, and the Gaza Division headquarters temporarily lost contact with many frontline positions. From the perspective of traditional command-and-control theory, these developments should have severely degraded the ability of Israeli forces to respond effectively.

Yet the empirical record suggests a different dynamic. Within minutes of the attack, Israeli soldiers, police officers, intelligence personnel, reservists, and local security teams began engaging Hamas fighters across a wide geographic area. Many of these engagements occurred without centralized coordination, and some involved individuals who had not received formal orders to deploy. Rather than waiting for communication systems to be restored, these responders began organizing local defense efforts and forming ad hoc teams with personnel from other units and agencies.

This pattern raises an important theoretical question. If the collapse of centralized command normally produces organizational paralysis, how did Israeli security forces generate such a rapid and decentralized response? Answering this question requires shifting attention from formal command structures to the **institutional characteristics of the responding forces themselves**. In particular, it requires examining whether certain organizational systems contain embedded capacities that allow them to function even when centralized coordination temporarily fails.

The concept of **Embedded Response Capacity (ERC)** proposed in this paper seeks to capture this phenomenon. ERC refers to the latent ability of trained personnel to transition rapidly from routine activity to autonomous combat response when formal command systems are disrupted. Rather than relying primarily on hierarchical direction, forces possessing ERC draw on shared training, tactical doctrine, and operational experience to coordinate their actions at the small-unit level.

Understanding ERC therefore requires examining how military and security organizations cultivate distributed competence among their personnel. In systems where tactical knowledge and decision-making authority are widely dispersed, the loss of centralized command may not eliminate the ability to respond. Instead, it may trigger a temporary shift from hierarchical coordination to decentralized action by semi-autonomous actors.

The following section develops the theoretical foundations of this concept by examining the literature on organizational resilience and latent organizing.

## **3\. Organizational Resilience and the Redundancy Paradox**

The puzzle identified in the previous section—how to achieve an effective decentralized response following the collapse of command-and-control systems—can be addressed through the literature on **organizational resilience**. Across disciplines such as sociology, management studies, and safety science, scholars have examined how complex organizations maintain functionality under conditions of extreme disruption. Much of this research focuses on systems that must operate in high-risk environments where failure carries catastrophic consequences, including nuclear power plants, air traffic control systems, and military organizations.

One of the most influential approaches to resilience in such environments is **High Reliability Organization (HRO) theory**. HRO research emerged from studies of organizations that manage hazardous technologies yet maintain remarkably low accident rates. According to Weick, Sutcliffe, and colleagues, these organizations cultivate specific organizing principles that enable them to function reliably even when confronted with uncertainty and operational surprises. These principles include a preoccupation with failure, reluctance to simplify interpretations, sensitivity to ongoing operations, commitment to resilience, and deference to expertise. Together, these characteristics produce what the authors describe as **collective mindfulness**, an organizational posture that allows actors throughout the system to detect emerging threats and respond quickly.

While HRO theory was not developed specifically for military organizations, several of its insights are relevant for understanding security forces operating in highly volatile environments. In particular, the principle of **deference to expertise** implies that decision authority should migrate toward individuals with the most relevant situational knowledge rather than remaining strictly centralized within hierarchical command structures. Under routine conditions, this migration may be limited; however, when unexpected disruptions occur, effective organizations allow expertise at lower levels to guide action. This dynamic enables rapid responses even when formal command channels are temporarily degraded.

A complementary perspective is offered by **latent organizing theory**, which focuses on how preexisting networks of trained individuals can activate rapidly in response to emergencies. Van Fenema and Romme describe latent organizing as a system in which individuals who normally operate in separate organizational roles can quickly form temporary teams when crises emerge. These teams rely on shared training, compatible procedures, and prior familiarity with similar tasks. Because the underlying competencies already exist within the system, the activation of such teams does not require detailed planning or centralized coordination. Instead, the organization draws upon a reservoir of distributed expertise that can be mobilized when needed.

Research on resilience in defense and security environments reinforces these insights. Systematic studies of military and emergency response organizations highlight the importance of **individual-level competencies**—including adaptive decision-making, psychological resilience, and shared tactical knowledge—in enabling effective responses under uncertainty. These attributes allow personnel to interpret ambiguous situations, coordinate informally with others, and improvise solutions when standard procedures are unavailable. Importantly, such capacities are not purely individual traits; they are cultivated through training regimes, professional norms, and operational experience.

Taken together, these strands of literature suggest that resilience is not solely a function of technological robustness or redundancy in physical infrastructure. Instead, it often depends on the **distribution of expertise and decision-making capability throughout the organization**. Systems that concentrate knowledge and authority at the top may function efficiently under normal conditions but become fragile when communication networks fail. By contrast, systems that disperse competence among many actors can continue operating even when hierarchical coordination is temporarily disrupted.

This observation leads to what can be described as the **redundancy paradox** in military organizations. Traditional conceptions of redundancy emphasize duplication of equipment, personnel, or command structures in order to ensure continuity of operations. However, redundancy can also exist in the form of **distributed human capability**. When training, doctrine, and operational knowledge are widely shared across units and agencies, multiple actors become capable of performing similar tactical functions. In such systems, the loss of centralized coordination does not eliminate the organization’s ability to act; rather, it shifts initiative toward actors operating closer to the point of contact.

The redundancy paradox therefore reflects a tension inherent in hierarchical systems. On the one hand, centralized command enhances coordination and strategic coherence. On the other hand, excessive dependence on centralized control can create vulnerabilities when communication networks are disrupted. Organizations that resolve this tension successfully are those that combine hierarchical coordination with **embedded decentralized competence**. In these systems, frontline personnel retain the capacity to act independently while still operating within a shared doctrinal and cultural framework.

The Israeli case provides an opportunity to examine this dynamic empirically. The events of October 7 occurred under conditions that closely resemble the scenarios discussed in resilience literature: communication networks were degraded, situational awareness was fragmented, and centralized coordination was temporarily impaired. Yet rather than producing widespread paralysis, the system generated numerous localized responses initiated by individuals and small groups. Personnel from different organizations—military units, police forces, intelligence services, and local defense teams—began engaging attackers across multiple locations, often forming ad hoc teams in the absence of formal coordination.

These observations suggest that the Israeli security system may possess a form of **institutionalized redundancy embedded within its human capital and training structures**. Tactical knowledge, operational experience, and decision-making authority appear to be sufficiently distributed among personnel that action can occur even without direct orders from higher headquarters. The resulting responses resemble the latent organizing mechanisms described in organizational theory, in which preexisting competencies allow rapid activation of temporary teams during emergencies.

To capture this phenomenon more precisely, the present study introduces the concept of **Embedded Response Capacity (ERC)**. ERC refers to the latent ability of security personnel to initiate coordinated tactical responses under conditions where formal command-and-control systems are disrupted. Rather than relying exclusively on hierarchical direction, actors possessing ERC draw on shared doctrine, training, and operational experience to organize and execute responses at the small-unit level.

In this sense, ERC represents a **field-level manifestation of organizational resilience**. It operationalizes several principles identified in the resilience literature—particularly deference to expertise, adaptability, and distributed competence—within the context of combat operations. The following section develops this concept in greater detail and outlines the mechanisms through which ERC may shape responses to large-scale attacks such as those observed on October 7\.

## **4\. Embedded Response Capacity (ERC): Conceptual Framework**

The previous section established that organizational resilience may emerge when expertise and operational competence are distributed throughout an organization rather than concentrated exclusively within hierarchical command structures. Building on this insight, the concept of **Embedded Response Capacity (ERC)** seeks to explain how security organizations can generate effective tactical responses even when centralized command-and-control systems temporarily fail.[^3]

ERC refers to the **latent ability of trained personnel within a security system to initiate and coordinate tactical action without direct orders from higher command**. This capacity does not arise spontaneously in moments of crisis; rather, it is embedded within the institutional characteristics of the organization. Training regimes, doctrinal principles, operational experience, and personnel structures collectively create a distributed reservoir of competence that can be activated when centralized coordination becomes unavailable.

In hierarchical organizations such as military forces, operational effectiveness is typically achieved through centralized planning and coordinated execution. However, when communication networks collapse or headquarters lose situational awareness, hierarchical coordination may become temporarily impossible. Under these conditions, organizations possessing ERC can shift from a **command-driven model of response** to a **distributed model of action** in which initiative is exercised at the level of individuals and small units. Personnel respond to local threats using shared tactical knowledge and operational norms, often forming ad hoc teams with members of other units or agencies.

ERC therefore operates as a mechanism that allows security organizations to transition between two modes of coordination. In routine circumstances, operations follow hierarchical command structures that ensure strategic coherence. When these structures are disrupted, however, embedded competencies enable decentralized actors to maintain operational activity until centralized coordination can be restored. This capacity transforms what might otherwise produce organizational paralysis into a distributed form of adaptive response.

Several institutional conditions appear particularly important for generating such capacity. The first concerns **shared tactical training**. When personnel across different units and organizations are trained in similar operational procedures—particularly in domains such as urban combat, close-quarters battle, and counterterrorism operations—they develop a common tactical language. This shared knowledge allows individuals who have not previously operated together to coordinate effectively in high-pressure environments.

The second condition involves **doctrinal emphasis on initiative at lower levels of command**. Military doctrines that encourage mission-type command—often described as “mission command”—prioritize the articulation of operational objectives while allowing subordinate actors discretion in determining how those objectives should be achieved. Such doctrines cultivate habits of independent decision-making and reinforce the expectation that personnel should act when confronted with emerging threats, even in the absence of explicit orders.

A third factor concerns the **distribution of operational experience across the force**. Security systems in which large numbers of personnel regularly participate in counterterrorism or internal security operations tend to diffuse tactical competence throughout the organization. Routine exposure to complex operational environments—particularly those involving urban combat or irregular threats—fosters familiarity with rapid decision-making under uncertainty.

Finally, the **composition of the personnel system** can influence the degree to which tactical expertise is dispersed throughout society. Systems that rely heavily on conscription and reserve service distribute military training across large segments of the population. Even when not formally mobilized, reservists retain the skills and knowledge necessary to respond to emergencies. This creates what might be described as a **latent operational network**, composed of trained individuals capable of mobilizing rapidly in response to security threats.

Taken together, these characteristics create the structural conditions for Embedded Response Capacity. In such systems, tactical competence is not limited to a small number of specialized units but is instead distributed across multiple organizations and personnel categories. When crises emerge, this dispersed expertise can be activated through decentralized initiative, allowing personnel to respond even before formal command structures have been reestablished.

The Israeli security system provides a particularly useful case for examining this phenomenon. Israel’s defense structure combines universal conscription, a large reserve component, and multiple professional security organizations—including military, police, and intelligence agencies—that routinely operate in overlapping counterterrorism environments. Many personnel across these organizations receive training in similar tactical procedures, particularly in urban combat and counterterrorism operations. Moreover, Israeli military doctrine has historically emphasized initiative and improvisation at the small-unit level, reflecting the operational realities faced by the state since its founding.

These structural characteristics suggest that the Israeli system may contain significant embedded response capacity. If so, the collapse of centralized command on October 7 should not necessarily have produced operational paralysis. Instead, one would expect to observe decentralized engagement by personnel possessing the training, experience, and initiative necessary to respond autonomously.

The empirical analysis presented in this paper seeks to evaluate this expectation by examining the composition and behavior of the forces that engaged Hamas fighters during the first stages of the attack. If ERC played a significant role in shaping the response, several observable patterns should emerge. First, a substantial portion of the responding forces should consist of individuals who joined the fighting independently rather than being formally deployed. Second, those individuals should disproportionately possess characteristics associated with embedded response capacity, such as officer status, special operations training, or reserve service. Third, locations where personnel from multiple organizations operated together should demonstrate evidence of rapid operational stabilization, reflecting the formation of ad hoc teams capable of coordinated action.

---

## **Figure 1\. Conceptual Model of Embedded Response Capacity**

![][image1]

**Institutional Foundations**

* Shared training systems

* Distributed professional expertise

* Interoperable operational protocols

⬇

**Embedded Response Capacity (ERC)**  
 Latent operational competence distributed across actors

⬇

**Crisis Condition**  
 Disruption of command-and-control systems

⬇

**Decentralized Operational Response**

* Self-mobilization of trained actors

* Ad hoc team formation

* Cross-organizational cooperation

⬇

**Outcome**  
 Organizational resilience and rapid stabilization

These expectations can be translated into a set of testable hypotheses. The next section outlines these hypotheses and specifies how they will be examined empirically.

## **5\. Hypotheses**

The concept of Embedded Response Capacity (ERC) developed in the previous section suggests that security organizations possessing widely distributed tactical competence and decentralized initiative should be able to generate effective responses even when centralized command structures collapse. If this argument is correct, the events of October 7 should reveal empirical patterns consistent with decentralized mobilization and ad hoc coordination among responding forces.

To evaluate this expectation, the study derives a set of testable hypotheses linking the institutional characteristics associated with ERC to observable patterns of battlefield response.

### **Two-Wave Response Hypothesis**

Large-scale surprise attacks typically confront security forces with an initial asymmetry of preparedness. Personnel stationed in the affected area must respond immediately to the attack, often under conditions of surprise and numerical disadvantage. Over time, however, additional responders may arrive from outside the initial combat zone once information about the attack spreads.

If decentralized initiative played an important role in the Israeli response, the mobilization of forces should occur in two distinct phases. The first phase consists of personnel already deployed along the Gaza border when the attack began. The second phase consists of personnel who arrived independently after learning about the unfolding events.

**H1: The Israeli response to the October 7 attack occurred in two distinguishable waves: (1) stationed personnel present in the combat zone at the onset of the attack and (2) responders who joined the fighting independently after the attack began.**

---

### **Voluntary Mobilization Hypothesis**

If Embedded Response Capacity enables decentralized initiative, a significant share of responding personnel should have joined the fighting without receiving formal deployment orders. In systems that depend heavily on centralized command, participation in combat operations is typically restricted to personnel formally assigned to the relevant operational sector. By contrast, systems with distributed tactical competence may generate spontaneous mobilization by trained individuals who recognize the threat and act independently.

**H2: A substantial proportion of responding forces joined the fighting independently rather than being formally stationed in the combat zone at the start of the attack.**

---

### **ERC Profile Hypothesis**

ERC assumes that decentralized response depends on the presence of individuals possessing relevant training, operational experience, and decision-making autonomy. Personnel who join the fighting independently should therefore display characteristics associated with higher levels of tactical competence and initiative. These characteristics may include officer status, membership in specialized units, or prior operational experience typical of reservists and professional soldiers.

**H3: Personnel who joined the fighting independently are more likely to possess characteristics associated with embedded response capacity—such as officer status, reserve service, or special operations training—than personnel stationed in the combat zone at the onset of the attack.**

---

### **Ad Hoc Team Formation Hypothesis**

A key mechanism through which ERC operates is the formation of temporary teams composed of personnel from different units and organizations. When individuals share common training, tactical doctrine, and operational norms, they can coordinate effectively even without prior planning or formal integration into the same command structure. This capacity should be observable in locations where personnel from multiple organizations engaged the attackers simultaneously.

**H4: Combat locations involving personnel from multiple organizational branches (e.g., military units, police forces, intelligence personnel, and local defense teams) will display evidence of ad hoc team formation.**

---

### **Operational Stabilization Hypothesis**

If ERC contributes to effective crisis response, the presence of decentralized initiative and cross-organizational cooperation should influence operational outcomes. In particular, locations where multiple trained actors responded rapidly should experience faster stabilization compared to locations where defensive forces were limited to the personnel initially stationed there.

**H5: Locations characterized by higher levels of decentralized participation and cross-organizational cooperation will achieve operational stabilization more rapidly than locations lacking these characteristics.**

---

Together, these hypotheses translate the theoretical concept of Embedded Response Capacity into observable implications that can be tested empirically. The following section describes the research design and methodological approach used to evaluate these hypotheses, including the construction of a novel dataset documenting the personnel who participated in the defense against the October 7 attack.

## **6\. Methodology**

### **Research Design**

This study employs a **mixed-method research design** combining quantitative analysis of original datasets with qualitative institutional analysis. The quantitative component reconstructs the composition, timing, and spatial distribution of the forces that responded to the October 7 attack. The qualitative component examines the institutional characteristics of the Israeli security system that may have produced Embedded Response Capacity (ERC).

The mixed-method approach is particularly appropriate for analyzing complex security events characterized by incomplete operational documentation. Large-scale terrorist attacks often generate chaotic conditions in which formal records of responding forces are fragmented or unavailable. By combining quantitative reconstruction of battlefield participation with qualitative examination of institutional structures, the study aims to identify both **observable patterns of response** and the **organizational mechanisms that produced them**.

---

### **Data Sources**

The empirical analysis relies on two original datasets constructed for the purposes of this study.

#### **Dataset 1: Combat Participation Dataset**

The first dataset reconstructs the **units and organizations that engaged Hamas forces during the October 7–8 battles**. Information was compiled from multiple open sources, including:

* media investigations and battlefield reconstructions  
* military and police archival materials  
* publicly available operational reports  
* documentation projects mapping the events of October 7

Each entry in the dataset records the **organizational unit involved in combat and the geographic location of engagement**. Locations include military bases, civilian communities, and transportation routes within the Gaza Envelope region. The dataset therefore provides a spatial overview of where different security organizations engaged the attackers.

This dataset allows the study to examine **organizational diversity at the location level**, a key indicator of ad hoc team formation.

---

#### **Dataset 2: Security Personnel Fatalities Dataset**

The second dataset documents **Israeli security personnel killed during the fighting on October 7 and 8, 2023**. Casualty data are used as a proxy for reconstructing the composition and behavior of responding forces.

Although casualty data do not capture all participants in combat operations, they provide a uniquely reliable source for identifying individuals who were actively engaged in fighting. Because fatalities are systematically documented by official institutions and widely reported in public records, they allow researchers to reconstruct participation patterns even when operational logs are unavailable.

The dataset was compiled by cross-referencing several official and public sources, including:

* the Israeli Ministry of Defense memorial database  
* Israel Police memorial records  
* public documentation projects mapping the October 7 attacks  
* verified media reports and family testimonies

The resulting dataset includes **369 security personnel fatalities** recorded during the first two days of the attack.

---

### **Variables and Coding**

For each individual in the fatalities dataset, several categories of variables were coded.

**Demographic Variables**

* age  
* gender

**Organizational Variables**

* organizational branch (IDF, Israel Police, Shin Bet, local defense teams)  
* specific unit identification

**Service Status**

* conscript  
* reservist  
* professional service member

**Operational Characteristics**

* officer status  
* membership in special operations or specialized counterterrorism units

**Spatial Variables**

* geographic location of death (community, base, or transport route)  
* geographic coordinates where available

**Temporal Variables**

* approximate time of death relative to the beginning of the attack

---

### **Key Analytical Variable: Mode of Joining the Fighting**

A central variable in the study captures **how individuals entered the combat zone**.

Each fatality was coded according to whether the individual:

* **Stationed (0)** – present in the operational sector as part of routine deployment when the attack began  
* **Self-Joined (1)** – arrived independently after learning about the attack or engaged without explicit operational orders

This distinction allows the study to examine whether decentralized initiative played a significant role in the response.

---

### **Location-Level Variables**

In addition to individual-level variables, the study constructs **location-level indicators** that capture the organizational composition of responding forces.

The most important of these indicators is **organizational diversity** at each combat location. This variable measures the number of distinct security organizations participating in fighting at a given site (e.g., military units, police forces, intelligence personnel, or local defense teams).

Higher levels of organizational diversity are interpreted as evidence of **ad hoc team formation**, a key mechanism through which Embedded Response Capacity may operate.

---

### **Analytical Strategy**

The hypotheses are evaluated using several complementary analytical approaches.

First, **descriptive temporal analysis** is used to identify patterns in the timing of fatalities. This analysis allows the study to determine whether two distinct waves of responders—stationed personnel and self-joining responders—can be observed.

Second, **frequency analysis** examines the proportion of individuals who joined the fighting independently. This provides an initial test of whether decentralized mobilization constituted a significant component of the response.

Third, **logistic regression analysis** is used to examine the characteristics associated with voluntary participation in the fighting. The dependent variable in this model is the binary indicator distinguishing self-joining responders from stationed personnel. Independent variables include officer status, service category, organizational affiliation, and membership in specialized units.

Finally, **location-level analysis** examines the relationship between organizational diversity and operational outcomes. By comparing locations with different levels of cross-organizational participation, the study assesses whether decentralized cooperation among multiple actors contributed to the restoration of operational control.

---

### **Methodological Contribution**

Beyond its substantive findings, the study introduces a methodological approach that may be useful for analyzing other complex security events. Using **fatality data as a proxy for reconstructing battlefield participation** allows researchers to examine response patterns even when detailed operational records are unavailable or classified. In environments characterized by spontaneous mobilization and incomplete documentation—conditions common in large-scale terrorist attacks—such indirect reconstruction provides a valuable tool for empirical analysis.

---

## **7\. Findings**

This section presents the empirical findings of the study. The analysis proceeds in four stages. First, descriptive statistics are used to reconstruct the timing of the Israeli response and to identify the presence of distinct waves of responders. Second, the composition of the responding forces is examined in order to assess the extent of decentralized participation. Third, statistical analysis evaluates the characteristics associated with voluntary mobilization. Finally, spatial analysis explores the formation of ad hoc teams across multiple organizations.

### **Temporal Structure of the Response**

The first hypothesis proposed that the Israeli response unfolded in two distinct phases: an initial wave composed of personnel already stationed in the combat zone, followed by a second wave of responders who joined the fighting independently after the attack began.

Temporal analysis of the fatalities dataset provides strong support for this expectation. Personnel stationed in the Gaza Envelope sector experienced the earliest casualties, reflecting their immediate engagement with attacking forces. The average time between the start of the attack and the deaths of these individuals was approximately four hours.

By contrast, individuals coded as **self-joining responders** tended to arrive later in the day, with an average time to death approximately two hours later than the stationed group. This temporal gap corresponds to the time required for information about the attack to spread and for individuals located outside the immediate combat zone to travel to the affected area.

These patterns suggest the existence of two analytically distinguishable waves of responders:  
(1) **stationed personnel** who absorbed the initial shock of the attack, and  
(2) **self-joining responders** who arrived subsequently and reinforced defensive efforts.

This finding provides empirical support for **H1**, the Two-Wave Response Hypothesis.

---

### **Decentralized Mobilization**

The second hypothesis predicted that a substantial portion of the responding forces joined the fighting independently rather than being formally deployed in the operational sector at the onset of the attack.

Analysis of the fatalities dataset indicates that decentralized mobilization played a significant role in the response. Among the 344 individuals identified as having actively engaged in combat operations, a large share were coded as **self-joining responders**. These individuals were not initially stationed along the Gaza border but arrived independently after learning about the attack.

Many of these responders traveled from other military bases, nearby towns, or private residences. Some arrived in uniform and carrying personal weapons, while others improvised equipment upon arrival. Despite the absence of formal deployment orders, they began engaging attacking forces and supporting defensive operations across multiple locations.

The prominence of self-joining responders supports **H2**, the Voluntary Mobilization Hypothesis, and suggests that decentralized initiative played an important role in the early stages of the response.

---

### **Characteristics of Self-Joining Responders**

The third hypothesis proposed that individuals who joined the fighting independently would be more likely to possess characteristics associated with Embedded Response Capacity, such as officer status, membership in specialized units, or reserve service.

Statistical comparison between stationed personnel and self-joining responders reveals several notable differences.

First, officers were substantially overrepresented among self-joining responders relative to stationed personnel. Officers typically receive more extensive tactical training and are expected to exercise independent judgment in rapidly evolving situations. Their disproportionate presence among voluntary responders is therefore consistent with the expectation that decentralized initiative is facilitated by higher levels of training and operational autonomy.

Second, membership in **specialized combat units**, including special operations forces and elite counterterrorism units, was also more common among self-joining responders. Personnel from such units often possess advanced training in small-unit tactics, urban combat, and rapid decision-making, all of which are critical under conditions where formal command structures are disrupted.

Third, **reservists** constituted a significant proportion of the self-joining group. Because reservists are geographically dispersed throughout society, they can mobilize rapidly when crises occur, even before formal call-up procedures are initiated. Their participation illustrates the role of distributed military expertise within the broader population.

Taken together, these findings support **H3**, the ERC Profile Hypothesis. Individuals who joined the fighting independently were more likely to possess the training, experience, and operational autonomy associated with embedded response capacity.

---

### **Ad Hoc Team Formation**

The fourth hypothesis focused on the formation of temporary teams composed of personnel from different organizations. If Embedded Response Capacity allows individuals with shared training and doctrine to coordinate without formal planning, then locations with multiple organizational participants should exhibit evidence of such ad hoc collaboration.

Spatial analysis of the combat participation dataset indicates that fighting occurred across **dozens of geographically dispersed locations**, including military bases, civilian communities, and transportation routes throughout the Gaza Envelope region. In many of these locations, personnel from multiple security organizations engaged attackers simultaneously.

Examples include engagements involving combinations of:

* Israel Defense Forces combat units  
* Israel Police officers and counterterrorism units  
* Shin Bet personnel operating in the area  
* local security teams and civilian emergency responders

The presence of multiple organizational actors within the same combat locations suggests that defensive efforts frequently relied on **cross-organizational cooperation** rather than strictly hierarchical coordination.

These patterns are consistent with **H4**, the Ad Hoc Team Formation Hypothesis.

---

### **Organizational Diversity and Operational Stabilization**

The final hypothesis proposed that locations characterized by decentralized participation and cross-organizational cooperation would experience faster stabilization.

Preliminary analysis of the combat locations suggests that areas where multiple trained actors engaged attackers tended to resist Hamas incursions more effectively than locations where defenders were limited to the personnel initially stationed there. The rapid emergence of ad hoc teams increased the density of defensive forces and generated multiple points of resistance across the battlespace.

Rather than confronting isolated defensive positions, attacking units encountered **a distributed network of responders** capable of engaging them across multiple locations. This pattern created friction for the attacking force and contributed to slowing its advance.

These findings provide initial support for **H5**, the Operational Stabilization Hypothesis, suggesting that embedded response capacity may have played a significant role in shaping the outcome of the early stages of the attack.

---

## **8\. Discussion**

The findings presented above provide empirical support for the central claim of this paper: that the Israeli response to the October 7 attack was shaped in part by the presence of **Embedded Response Capacity (ERC)** within the security system. Rather than producing the organizational paralysis predicted by traditional command-and-control theory, the collapse of communication networks and centralized coordination coincided with the rapid emergence of decentralized tactical responses across multiple locations.

This section interprets these findings in relation to the theoretical frameworks discussed earlier and considers their broader implications for understanding organizational resilience in security institutions.

### **Resolving the Command Collapse Puzzle**

A central puzzle motivating this study concerns the apparent contradiction between established theories of command and control and the empirical pattern observed on October 7\. Conventional models of military organization assume that effective operations depend on the integrity of hierarchical communication systems. When these systems fail, coordination becomes difficult, and units may hesitate to act independently. Under such conditions, the expectation is that operational effectiveness will deteriorate until communication links are restored.

The Israeli case appears to deviate from this expectation. Despite severe disruption to surveillance and communication systems along the Gaza border, security personnel began engaging attackers across a wide geographic area within a relatively short period of time. These engagements often occurred without centralized coordination and involved individuals who had not been formally deployed to the operational sector.

The empirical patterns identified in the findings section suggest that this decentralized response was not purely accidental or the product of individual improvisation. Instead, it appears to reflect structural characteristics of the Israeli security system that distribute tactical competence and decision-making authority across a wide range of personnel. These characteristics allowed trained actors to respond autonomously when centralized command structures were temporarily degraded.

In this sense, the Israeli case illustrates the **redundancy paradox** discussed earlier. Systems designed for hierarchical coordination may also contain embedded forms of distributed capability that become visible when centralized control fails. Rather than producing paralysis, the collapse of command networks can trigger a shift from hierarchical coordination to decentralized action.

### **ERC as a Mechanism of Organizational Resilience**

The concept of Embedded Response Capacity provides a framework for understanding how this shift occurs. ERC captures the latent ability of trained personnel to initiate tactical action under conditions of uncertainty, drawing on shared doctrine, training, and operational experience.

The empirical findings are consistent with several mechanisms through which ERC may operate.

First, the prominence of self-joining responders suggests that **initiative at the individual level** played a crucial role in the early stages of the response. Personnel who were not formally deployed to the combat zone nevertheless recognized the threat and moved toward the affected area. Their participation increased the density of defensive forces and expanded the number of locations where attackers encountered resistance.

Second, the overrepresentation of officers, reservists, and members of specialized units among self-joining responders indicates that **training and operational experience** facilitated decentralized initiative. Individuals possessing greater tactical expertise and decision-making autonomy were more likely to act independently when communication networks failed.

Third, the presence of multiple organizational actors within many combat locations suggests that decentralized responses frequently involved **ad hoc collaboration** among personnel from different agencies. Shared tactical knowledge and compatible operational procedures allowed these actors to coordinate effectively even in the absence of formal integration into a single command structure.

Together, these mechanisms illustrate how ERC can function as a form of organizational resilience embedded within the human capital of the security system.

### **Implications for Theories of Security Organizations**

The Israeli case contributes to a broader discussion in the literature concerning the relationship between hierarchy and adaptability in military organizations. Traditional command structures are designed to ensure coordination and strategic coherence across large forces. However, highly centralized systems may struggle to respond effectively when information flows are disrupted or when events unfold more rapidly than centralized decision-making processes can accommodate.

The findings presented here suggest that resilience may depend on the ability of security organizations to combine hierarchical coordination with **distributed competence**. Systems that cultivate initiative, shared tactical doctrine, and cross-organizational interoperability may be better able to adapt when centralized command temporarily fails.

This insight aligns with research on high-reliability organizations and complex adaptive systems, which emphasizes the importance of distributed expertise and flexible decision-making in uncertain environments. In such systems, resilience emerges not from rigid adherence to hierarchical procedures but from the capacity of actors throughout the organization to detect and respond to emerging threats.

### **The Role of Personnel Systems**

One institutional feature that appears particularly relevant for generating Embedded Response Capacity is the structure of the personnel system. Israel’s reliance on conscription and reserve service distributes military training across a large segment of the population. As a result, many individuals possess the skills necessary to participate in defensive operations even when they are not formally mobilized.

This structure effectively creates a **latent network of trained responders** embedded within society. When crises occur, these individuals can mobilize rapidly and contribute to defensive efforts before formal deployment processes are initiated.

While such systems may impose economic and administrative costs, the findings of this study suggest that they may also provide operational advantages in environments characterized by irregular threats and rapid escalation.

### **Limitations**

Several limitations should be acknowledged. First, the use of casualty data as a proxy for battlefield participation necessarily captures only a subset of all individuals who engaged in the fighting. Many participants survived and are therefore not represented in the dataset. While fatalities provide reliable evidence of engagement, they do not fully reconstruct the complete composition of responding forces.

Second, the analysis relies on open-source documentation and reconstructed timelines, which may contain gaps or inconsistencies. Although multiple sources were cross-referenced to improve accuracy, some uncertainty remains regarding the precise timing and sequence of events.

Finally, the Israeli case represents a single empirical context. While the findings suggest broader theoretical implications, additional comparative research would be necessary to determine whether similar dynamics operate in other security systems.

---

## **9\. Conclusion**

This paper examined how Israeli security forces responded to the large-scale Hamas attack of October 7, 2023, despite the disruption of communication networks and centralized command structures. Drawing on a novel dataset documenting the personnel who participated in the fighting, the study investigated whether decentralized initiative and cross-organizational cooperation contributed to the defense against the attack.

The findings indicate that the response was characterized by two distinguishable waves of responders: personnel stationed in the combat zone at the onset of the attack and individuals who joined the fighting independently after learning about the unfolding events. Many of the latter possessed characteristics associated with high levels of tactical competence, including officer status, reserve service, and membership in specialized units. Furthermore, engagements across multiple locations frequently involved personnel from different organizations forming ad hoc teams.

These patterns are consistent with the presence of **Embedded Response Capacity**, defined as the latent ability of trained actors to initiate coordinated tactical responses when formal command-and-control systems are disrupted. Rather than producing organizational paralysis, the collapse of centralized coordination activated decentralized initiative among personnel distributed throughout the security system.

By introducing the concept of ERC, this study contributes to the literature on organizational resilience and crisis response in security institutions. It suggests that resilience may depend not only on technological redundancy or logistical preparedness but also on the distribution of competence and decision-making authority throughout the force.

These insights have implications for how military and security organizations prepare for emerging threats such as swarm attacks and hybrid warfare. Systems that cultivate shared tactical knowledge, encourage initiative, and maintain interoperability across organizational boundaries may be better positioned to respond effectively when centralized command structures are temporarily degraded.

Future research should explore whether similar dynamics operate in other contexts, including different national security systems and other forms of large-scale terrorist or hybrid attacks. Understanding how decentralized capabilities interact with hierarchical command structures remains an important challenge for scholars and practitioners alike in an era of increasingly complex security threats.

[^1]:  Toth, F. (2025). Hamas’s October 7 attack: Analysis of an “antagonistic” crisis. *Public Administration Review*. https://doi.org/10.1111/puar.13955

[^2]:  Edri-Peer, O., Cohen, N., & Gilad, Z. (2025). Why do street-level bureaucrats risk their lives in times of crisis? The case of Israeli police officers during the October 7 attack. *American Review of Public Administration*, *55*(7–8), 615–626. https://doi.org/10.1177/02750740251361869

[^3]:  Embedded Response Capacity (ERC) refers to the latent operational capability that exists within a public security system when training, organizational structures, and professional norms distribute tactical competence across a wide range of actors rather than concentrating it exclusively in centralized command structures. Figure 1 illustrates the theoretical logic underlying ERC. Institutional characteristics—such as shared training frameworks, distributed professional expertise, and organizational interoperability—embed operational competence within individuals and units throughout the system. Under normal conditions, these competencies function within hierarchical command structures. However, when crises disrupt coordination mechanisms and communication systems, these embedded capabilities allow trained actors to initiate decentralized responses based on shared doctrine and situational awareness. The result is the rapid formation of ad hoc teams and distributed operational activity across the crisis environment, enabling the organization to maintain effectiveness even in the absence of centralized coordination. In this sense, ERC represents a mechanism through which public security organizations transform distributed expertise into organizational resilience under conditions of systemic disruption.
